Portfolio · Spring 2026

Mark K.

12 years old. Florida. I dig up Pliocene fossils, breed peppers, and build games with my sister.

Eagles Landing Middle School · Currently applying to Pine Crest

About

What I'm doing right now

I split my time across three projects that don't look related on paper — a game and music platform I run with my sister, a Florida raised-bed garden where I'm planning a pepper-breeding project, and a mineral collection focused on a single, unusual Florida fossil locality. The connecting thread is that I like doing things, not just reading about them.

I'm most curious about artificial intelligence, entrepreneurship, and STEM. AI is already part of how I build — I use Microsoft Copilot for web work and Google AI Studio for game development. The skill I'm working on is directing AI well: the ideas, design decisions, and debugging are mine; the code is collaborative.

Project · Live

MGC NEXUS

A games and music sharing platform I'm building with my sister. MGC stands for Music Games Center — a single place for indie developers and musicians to publish work and find an audience.

  • My role on the site: came up with the concept and feature set, designed the brand, chose the layout and functionality, fixed a lot of bugs, and deployed everything to Cloudflare. I used Microsoft Copilot to help build it — I made the product decisions; Copilot helped with the code.
  • My games: I came up with all the ideas myself — Zenith Packs (a complex game, more than just an RNG opener) and the in-development Zenith Packs Garden. I use Google AI Studio to help me turn those ideas into working code.
  • My sister's role: the music side of the platform.
  • What I'm learning from it: how to direct AI tools well. The ideas and design decisions are mine, the code is collaborative.
Visit MGCNEXUS.com →

Specialty · Mineralogy

Ruck's Pit calcite clams

I collect minerals and fossils, and my specialty is the "crystal clams" of Ruck's Pit in Fort Drum, Florida — one of very few localities in the world where Pliocene-era fossil clams (Mercenaria sp., roughly 2–3 million years old) are filled with golden calcite crystals that formed inside the empty shell cavity as calcium-rich groundwater seeped in.

So far I've personally extracted 18–19 crystal clams, and my full mineral collection is around 80 specimens including quartz, amethyst, and citrine.

Left: golden calcite (iron-stained). Middle: an intact specimen — every dig is essentially a coin-flip on what's inside. Right: a lighter, near-colorless variation — calcite color shifts with the trace iron content of the groundwater when the crystals formed.

How I tell calcite from quartz in the field: calcite is much softer (Mohs ~3, scratches with a copper coin) and fizzes when you touch it with dilute acid like vinegar. Quartz does neither.

Project · Hands-on science

Florida pepper-breeding project

I run a raised-bed garden where I'm experimenting with vegetables in the Florida climate. This summer I'm starting a hand-pollinated Capsicum breeding project — using bell, jalapeño, and banana-type peppers (all C. annuum) to see if I can produce an F1 with the heat profile of one parent and the fruit shape of another. Florida's long growing season actually lets me get more generations per year than most US climates.

Left: this bell pepper is fasciated — its flower fused extra carpels during development. I'm tracking whether the plant produces fasciated fruit consistently (possibly genetic) or just once (environmental). Right: red jalapeños — same plant as green ones, but left on the vine until fully ripe, which raises capsaicin and sugar. They're what dried chipotle peppers are made from.

Cherry tomatoes ripening in stages on a single truss — green → breaker → red. Florida's biggest challenge is that tomato pollen goes sterile above ~90°F daytime, so summer fruit-set drops sharply.

Workshop

3D printing

I have a 3D printer at home and I'm experimenting with what's actually worth selling. Most people sell undifferentiated stuff on Etsy, so I'm looking at niches where I can combine my hobbies — like custom mineral display stands or garden-tool parts — and pick one product to test this summer.

Curiosity · Finance & Entrepreneurship

Business and investments

I'm seriously interested in how businesses actually get built, so I attend tech and business conferences locally. The photo below is from Cases & Faces — International Tech & Business Conference and Award, part of the South Florida innovation ecosystem.

I'm also learning about the markets — I follow stocks and have started investing small amounts in cryptocurrency to understand how the systems actually work, not to chase quick wins.

What I'm exploring

Right now, I'm trying to learn more about

  • Artificial intelligence — how to build with it, not just use it
  • Entrepreneurship and investing — including how the stock market actually works
  • Game design — especially what makes a game more than just RNG
  • Florida geology and horticulture — the local context for both my hobbies
